content:
The strongest argument for keeping SSH stubborn, weird, and separate was written in a CVE from 2014.

8 min read
Just now

--

Every year or so the same question finds me again — in a design review, in a hallway argument, in the back of my own head while I stare at yet another fingerprint prompt I’m about to accept without reading: why doesn’t SSH just run over TLS?
And I’ll be honest with you. For years I quietly suspected the question was smarter than the protocol.
On paper it sounds like overdue cleanup. One cryptographic stack instead of two. Real certificates from a real authority instead of hex strings nobody verifies. Mutual TLS, SNI, automated issuance — twenty-five years of web-scale hardening, sitting right there, already paid for.
For a long time I assumed the only honest answer was inertia. SSH is older than TLS. Migrations hurt. Nobody volunteers to write that RFC.
Then I went back and read the timeline of April 2014 properly, and something in me shifted. The answer stopped being about inertia and started being about survival.
